explanation: The natural language states that whenever 'standby' is true, then eventually, the system must enter the 'state_is_STANDBY' state from the 'state_is_NOMINAL' state. This is captured using the implication operator -> and the F (eventually) operator.

The natural language indicates that if the pilot is in control, i.e., 'standby' is true, then the autopilot changes from NOMINAL to STANDBY. This can be interpreted as when 'standby' is true, it implies that 'state_is_NOMINAL' is false in the next state and 'state_is_STANDBY' is true in the next state.

The natural language indicates that if the pilot is in control (standby), then eventually the autopilot changes states from NOMINAL to STANDBY. This translates to globally, if standby is true, then eventually state_is_STANDBY is true and state_is_NOMINAL becomes false.

The natural language indicates a transition from NOMINAL to STANDBY when the pilot is in control.  This is expressed using an implication: if the pilot is in control (standby), then it must be the case that the autopilot is in the STANDBY state and the previous state was NOMINAL.

The LTL formula 'G(standby -> X(state_is_STANDBY))' means that globally, if the pilot is in control (standby), then in the next state, the autopilot will be in STANDBY state. This directly captures the requirement that the autopilot transitions from NOMINAL to STANDBY when the pilot is in control.

The natural language indicates that when the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot will change states from NOMINAL to STANDBY. This can be represented as globally, if the pilot is in control (standby), then eventually the autopilot state will be STANDBY. Note that the change from NOMINAL to STANDBY is captured by eventually being in STANDBY.

The natural language indicates that if 'standby' is true, then 'state_is_NOMINAL' should change to 'state_is_STANDBY'. This is captured by the global property that if 'standby' is true, then next 'state_is_NOMINAL' implies next 'state_is_STANDBY'.

The natural language indicates that whenever the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot should change from NOMINAL to STANDBY. This can be represented using the globally (G) operator and implication (->).

The natural language indicates a state transition from NOMINAL to STANDBY upon the pilot's control (standby). This can be expressed using the globally (G) operator and the implication (->) operator to ensure that whenever standby is true, it implies that in the next state (X), the system will be in the STANDBY state, and also implies that the system was in the NOMINAL state.

The natural language states that whenever the pilot is in control (standby is true), the autopilot changes from NOMINAL to STANDBY. This can be represented as a global property: always (standby -> next(state_is_STANDBY)). It is also important to capture the switch from NOMINAL, which is why we say that globally, whenever standby is true, it should be the case that eventually the state is standby and before that, we are in NOMINAL state

The natural language indicates that whenever the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot should transition from NOMINAL to STANDBY. Thus, we use the implication operator -> with 'standby' as the antecedent and 'X state_is_STANDBY' as the consequent, wrapped within a G operator to enforce this globally. Note that the consequent also needs to assert that we were in NOMINAL, i.e. G(state_is_NOMINAL -> X state_is_STANDBY). Therefore, combining this with the given condition, we get G(standby & state_is_NOMINAL -> X state_is_STANDBY).

The natural language states that when 'standby' is true, the system shall change states from 'state_is_NOMINAL' to 'state_is_STANDBY'. This can be represented as G(standby -> X(state_is_STANDBY)) & G(standby -> !state_is_NOMINAL) , where the first part of the conjunction ensures that eventually the system is in state STANDBY when 'standby' becomes true, and the second part ensures that the system is not in state NOMINAL.

The LTL formula 'G(standby -> X(state_is_STANDBY))' means that globally, if the pilot is in control (standby), then in the next state, the autopilot should be in the STANDBY state. The formula 'G(state_is_NOMINAL -> !standby U state_is_STANDBY)' states that globally, if the autopilot is in the NOMINAL state, then the autopilot will remain in the NOMINAL state UNTIL the pilot takes control (standby) at which point the autopilot will be in the STANDBY state. Combining these two statements creates an appropriate formalization.
